_about: Cross-deck SYNTHESIS for each Major Arcanum — the evolution narrative across all decks. Keyed by trump_key. Consumed by (1) scripts/build_meta_grammar.py, which injects it into the emergent num-major-N nodes as an 'Across the decks' section, and (2) viewers/prototypes/lenses.html, which renders it as an overview header. Add a trump here and both surfaces pick it up. Stubs left empty fall back to the auto-generated one-liner.
fool: Across the decks, the Fool travels further than any other card — not in its picture, which stays remarkably stable, but in its rank and meaning. The figure is recognisable across five centuries: a wanderer in motley with a staff and bundle over his shoulder and an animal at his legs. The **Visconti-Sforza** (1451) shows him as a ragged youth, feathers in his hair and tattered clothing — the 'wild man' or madman of medieval iconography. The **Marseille** (1760) fixes the canonical image: the parti-coloured vagrant striding to the card's edge, a dog tearing at his leg. In the card *game* he was the **excuse** — the one card belonging to no suit, worth almost nothing, yet playable at any time to escape following suit. Then the meaning inverts. **Court de Gébelin** (1781) reads the unnumbered Fool as **Zero** — nothing in itself, yet the cipher that gives the other cards their value. The English occultists complete the turn: in the **Golden Dawn** and Rider-Waite-Smith (1909) the same motley youth now stands at the edge of a cliff, a white rose in hand and a white dog at his heels, about to step into the abyss — innocence, the soul before its descent, the alpha and omega of the Major Arcana. The card that was worth nothing in a game became the spiritual zero-point of an esoteric cosmos, while keeping its dog, its bundle, and its forward step the whole way.
death: Death is the clearest case of an image holding while its meaning is rewritten. The skeleton is constant; everything around it shifts. The oldest deck, the **Cary-Yale** (1442), shows Death *mounted, carrying a bow* — the archer who shoots down the living, rendered with courtly gold-ground dignity in the tradition of Petrarch's *Triumph of Death*. By the printed **Marseille** (1760) the attribute has changed to the **scythe**: Death the reaper, mowing a field from which crowned heads, hands and feet sprout — and the card is numbered **XIII but left unnamed**, a printers' convention. The meaning across the early decks is the same blunt one, shared with the popular *danse macabre*: Death is the great leveller, sparing no rank. Then the occultists transform it. **Court de Gébelin** (1781) reads the blank name as cosmic significance ('Death belongs to no rank'); **Wirth** and the **Golden Dawn** (1889–1909) re-key the card to Scorpio and the Hebrew letter Nun, insist it means **transformation, not extinction**, and put Death back on a *horse* — now with a banner promising sunrise. Mounted to on-foot to mounted; bow to scythe to banner; mortal allegory to great leveller to esoteric rebirth — the figure stayed recognisable for five centuries while its meaning was completely remade.
magician: The Magician's table stays the same for five hundred years; the man behind it is reinvented. In the **Visconti-Sforza** (1451) he is a seated craftsman or merchant at a bench of small objects. The **Marseille** (1760) names him **Le Bateleur** — a street conjurer or cup-and-ball trickster, the lowest-status of the trumps, his table set with the swindler's tools. The first occultists turn the swindler into a sage: **Court de Gébelin** (1781) read the Bateleur as an Egyptian magus, and the **Golden Dawn** / Rider-Waite-Smith (1909) complete it — the objects on his table become the four suit emblems (cup, wand, sword, pentacle, the elements themselves), one hand raised to heaven and one to earth. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Beth** and the planet **Mercury**, the carnival cheat becomes the Hermetic magician who works *as above, so below*.
high-priestess: Of all the trumps the second is the most theologically scandalous and the most disguised. The early Italian decks show a crowned woman in **papal robes** holding a book — the **Papesse**, the 'female pope,' echoing the legend of Pope Joan. The **Marseille** keeps her, tiara and open book, in Marian blue and white. So contentious was the image that the Swiss **Besançon** and **Belgian** decks replaced her outright with **Juno** and her peacock. The occultists rescued and re-coded her: stripped of the papal reference she becomes the **High Priestess** seated between two pillars (the black Boaz and white Jachin of Solomon's temple), guarding a hidden scroll. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Gimel** and the **Moon**, the forbidden female pope becomes the keeper of inner, lunar mystery — the hidden counterpart to the visible Empress.
empress: The Empress is among the most stable trumps in image and the most expanded in meaning. From the **Cary-Yale** and **Visconti** (1440s–50s) she is a crowned imperial woman, often with the **eagle** shield of the Holy Roman Empire and the orb and sceptre of temporal sovereignty. The **Marseille** keeps these regalia. The occultists shift her from a *political* to a *generative* power: the **Golden Dawn** / RWS seat her not in a court but in a lush field, robed in **pomegranates**, the crowned mother of natural abundance. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Daleth** (the Door) and the planet **Venus**, the earthly Empress becomes the principle of fertility and creative nature herself — the fruitful, visible counterpart to the hidden High Priestess.
emperor: The Emperor's evolution mirrors his consort's: from temporal ruler to cosmic principle. The **Cary-Yale** and **Visconti** show a crowned sovereign with the imperial **eagle**, painted in Bonifacio Bembo's courtly style; the **Marseille** keeps the eagle-shield and the profile pose of a man of state. The occult tradition abstracts him into the archetype of **structuring authority**: the **Golden Dawn** / RWS place him on a stone throne carved with **ram's heads**, holding an **ankh** sceptre. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Heh** (the Window) and the sign **Aries** — cardinal fire, initiative — the political emperor becomes the principle of order, law, and the rational will that gives the world its form.
hierophant: The fifth trump is the Emperor's spiritual twin, and like the Papesse it carried papal charge. The early decks and the **Marseille** show **Le Pape** — an enthroned pope in the triple tiara, hand raised in blessing, two tonsured figures before him. The same Reformation-era discomfort that turned the Papesse into Juno turned the Pope into **Jupiter** in the **Besançon** and **Belgian** decks. The occultists renamed him the **Hierophant** — the initiator of the Greek mysteries — seated between two pillars as the bridge between heaven and the seeker. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Vav** (the Nail that joins) and the sign **Taurus**, the institutional Pope becomes the teacher of sacred tradition — the keeper of the outer doctrine that the High Priestess holds within.
lovers: The sixth trump changed not only its meaning but its very subject. The **Cary-Yale** shows a courtly **betrothal** — a noble couple, a marriage between dynasties. The **Marseille** turns it into a **choice**: a young man stands between **two women** while Cupid draws his bow above — the crossroads of vice and virtue. The **Golden Dawn** / RWS rewrite the scene as **Adam and Eve** in the Garden, the Tree of Knowledge and its serpent behind the woman, an angel above. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Zayin** (the Sword that discriminates) and the sign **Gemini** — the twins, duality — the wedding portrait becomes the archetype of union, choice, and the splitting of the one into two.
chariot: The Chariot is triumph made visible, and its rider rises in dignity over time. The **Cary-Yale** shows a victor in a triumphal car, in the literal spirit of the *trionfi* — the Petrarchan procession of Triumphs that gave tarot its name. The **Marseille** crowns and armours him in a stone canopied vehicle drawn by two horses. The occultists cosmologise the vehicle: the **Golden Dawn** / RWS replace the horses with two **sphinxes, one black and one white** — the charioteer mastering opposed forces by will alone. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Cheth** (the enclosure) and the sign **Cancer**, the parade-float conqueror becomes the soul that holds contradictory drives in a single forward course.
strength: Strength is the card whose very *number* migrated, and the story is in the move. As the virtue **Fortitude** the **Cary-Yale** shows the figure breaking a column (the Samson motif); the **Marseille** softens it to a woman calmly **opening a lion's mouth** with bare hands — mastery without violence. The decisive change is positional: in the older order Strength sat at **XI**, but the **Golden Dawn** swapped it with Justice to **VIII** to align the trumps with the zodiac — Strength to **Leo**, the lion itself. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Teth** (the Serpent of coiled power) and crowned with the **lemniscate** of infinity, the cardinal virtue becomes the gentle, inexhaustible force that tames the animal nature by love. (See Justice for the other half of the swap.)
hermit: The ninth trump began as **Time** and became **Wisdom** — the single most telling change in the deck. The earliest decks show an old man carrying an **hourglass** — *Il Tempo*, Old Father Time, the *memento mori* of the *trionfi*. By the **Marseille** the hourglass has become a **lantern** half-shrouded in his cloak: the solitary sage lighting the way. The **Golden Dawn** / RWS set him on a **mountain peak**, the lantern enclosing a six-pointed star. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Yod** (the Hand, the seed-point of every other letter) and the sign **Virgo**, the figure of devouring Time becomes the inner guide whose small light is the first principle from which understanding grows.
wheel-of-fortune: The Wheel is the one trump that was already a complete medieval allegory before tarot adopted it. The early decks show blindfolded **Fortuna** turning her wheel, with human figures **rising, reigning, falling, and crushed** at its four quarters — the *regnabo, regno, regnavi, sum sine regno* of countless manuscripts. The **Marseille** keeps the turning wheel and its clinging figures. The occultists fix and cosmologise it: the **Golden Dawn** / RWS plant a **sphinx** with a sword at the summit — the still point above the turning — and inscribe the rim with letters. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Kaph** (the grasping palm) and the planet **Jupiter**, the emblem of blind chance becomes the law of cycles and the providence that turns beneath all fortune.
justice: Justice is the other half of the great renumbering. The early Italian decks and the **Marseille** show the classical virtue plainly — an enthroned woman with the **upright sword** and **scales** — seated at **VIII**. To align the trumps with the zodiac the **Golden Dawn** moved Justice to **XI** (swapping with Strength), keying it to **Libra**, the scales in the sky. Otherwise the image is the most conservative in the deck: sword and balance, unchanged from antiquity. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Lamed** (the ox-goad, the teacher's prod), the cardinal virtue keeps its plain meaning — equilibrium, law, the weighing of act against consequence — while quietly showing how the occultists bent the historical sequence to fit a cosmological scheme. (See Strength for the swap's other side.)
hanged-man: No trump's meaning depends more on the eye of the reader. The **Marseille** *Le Pendu* shows a young man suspended **by one foot** from a wooden frame, hands bound — yet his face is **calm**, almost serene. In its Italian context the image likely evoked the *pittura infamante*, the shame-portrait of a **traitor** hung by the heel; but the peaceful expression always invited the opposite reading. The occultists made that inversion the whole point: the **Golden Dawn** / RWS halo the head and read the suspension as **voluntary sacrifice and reversed vision** — wisdom gained by seeing the world upside down. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Mem** (Water, a mother letter), the traitor's punishment becomes the mystic's surrender — suspension between worlds, the self let go.
temperance: Temperance is the angel of measured flow. From the early decks through the **Marseille** the image is consistent: a **winged figure pouring liquid between two vessels** in a thin, unbroken stream — the cardinal virtue of right mixture. The occultists deepen it into an alchemical operation: the **Golden Dawn** / RWS stand the angel with **one foot on land and one in water**, pouring the eternal tincture between cups — the reconciliation of opposites. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Samekh** (the prop or support) and the sign **Sagittarius** — the arrow aimed higher — the homely virtue of moderation becomes the art of dynamic balance, the patient blending by which spirit is distilled from matter.
devil: The Devil is missing from the oldest surviving deck — no Visconti-Sforza Devil survives, and its look is inferred from the standard programme. By the **Marseille** the type is fixed: a **bat-winged**, horned figure on a pedestal, torch in hand, two small horned figures often chained below. The early meaning is the plain Christian one — bondage to sin, the adversary. The occultists widen it from theology to psychology: the **Golden Dawn** / RWS keep the chained pair but make the chains **loose**, voluntary — bondage to matter that the captives could slip if they chose. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Ayin** (the Eye of outer perception) and the sign **Capricorn**, the medieval Satan becomes the archetype of materialism, compulsion, and the shadow self.
tower: Like the Devil, the Tower does not survive in the earliest decks and is reconstructed from the type. Its **Marseille** name is the strange and revealing one — **La Maison-Dieu**, the 'House of God' — a **tower struck by a bolt from the sky**, its crown knocked off, two figures falling. The catastrophe was read variously: the Tower of Babel, the fall of pride, the lightning of divine correction. The occultists keep the image almost unchanged — the **Golden Dawn** / RWS show the same struck tower, falling crown, and tumbling bodies — and read it as the sudden collapse of false structures. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Peh** (the Mouth, speech and destruction) and the planet **Mars**, the House of God becomes the shock that breaks what was built wrong — necessary, clarifying ruin.
star: The Star quietly disrobes across the centuries. The early decks show a clothed, upright woman **holding a star aloft** like a standard. The **Marseille** transforms her into the canonical scene the occultists inherit: a **nude woman kneeling** at a pool, **pouring water from two vessels** — one onto the land, one into the stream — beneath **eight stars**. The **Golden Dawn** / RWS keep this almost exactly. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Tzaddi** (the fish-hook drawing up what lies beneath) and the sign **Aquarius**, the water-bearer, the ceremonial star-holder becomes the image of **hope** after the Tower's ruin: the soul replenishing the world from an inexhaustible source.
moon: The Moon gathers its eerie machinery over time. The earliest decks show simply a **woman holding a crescent**. The **Marseille** assembles the uncanny scene: a great **moon-face** dripping dew, **two towers**, a **dog and a wolf** baying, and a **crayfish** crawling from the pool — a landscape of the threshold and the night-mind. The **Golden Dawn** / RWS keep every element, reading them as the path through the subconscious between the towers. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Qoph** (the back of the head, the subconscious) and the sign **Pisces**, the lady-with-crescent becomes the card of **illusion, dream, and the tidal pull of the unconscious** — the difficult passage crossed in the dark.
sun: The Sun grows brighter and more human as it travels. An early type shows a **putto flying with a sun-disc**; the **Marseille** sets a great **radiant sun-face** over **two children** playing by a low wall — innocence under a beneficent light. The **Golden Dawn** / RWS distil the children to a single **naked child on a white horse** beneath sunflowers, banner raised. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Resh** (the Head, the illuminated intellect) and the **Sun** itself, the card is one of the few whose meaning barely shifts: from the earliest decks to the occult ones it is **clarity, life, and conscious joy** — the trumps' one card of unambiguous good.
judgement: Judgement keeps its Christian scene and gradually loosens its doctrine. From the **Cary-Yale** through the **Marseille** the image is the **Last Judgment**: an **angel sounding a trumpet** above the **dead rising from their graves** — pope, emperor, and commoner together. The occultists retain the resurrection but shift its key from final judgment to **awakening and rebirth**: the **Golden Dawn** / RWS show the same angel and rising figures, now read as the call to a higher life. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Shin** (Fire, a mother letter), the Christian eschaton becomes the card of **summons, renewal, and the answering of an inner call** — the self rising to a new octave.
world: The World is the seal of the deck, and it changes least because it was already complete. The **Cary-Yale** shows a cosmological emblem of the perfected universe; the **Marseille** fixes the enduring image — a **dancing nude figure within a laurel wreath** (the mandorla), framed at the four corners by the **four living creatures** (angel, eagle, lion, bull — the fixed signs, the evangelists, the four elements). The **Golden Dawn** / RWS keep it almost intact, the dancer holding two wands. Re-keyed to the Hebrew letter **Tau** (the Cross, the final letter) and the planet **Saturn**, the medieval image of cosmic order becomes the card of **completion and integration** — the end of the Fool's journey that began at zero.
